nodes.context: Far-left political organization that developed from a Chicago street gang, active in Chicago and New York City.
Revolutionary socialist organization that inspired the Young Lords and allied with them.
Activist organization advocating for Latin American unity and structural change.
Socialist organization that collaborated with the Young Lords in building occupations.
Student activist organization that collaborated with the Young Lords.
Successor organization to the Young Lords Party, formed in 1972.
Socialist political party that some YLP members joined after the YLP dissolved.
Far-left political organization that developed from a Chicago street gang, active in Chicago and New York City, known for direct action and community programs.
Revolutionary socialist organization that inspired the Young Lords and collaborated with them.
Organization that collaborated with the Young Lords Party to form the Think Lincoln Committee.
Local organization in Bridgeport, Connecticut that merged with the Young Lords to form the Bridgeport chapter.
Local group in Philadelphia from which the Philadelphia Young Lords emerged.
Established nationalist group in Puerto Rico that criticized the Young Lords Party.
Puerto Rican socialist party that some Young Lords members joined after the split.
Far-left political organization that developed from a Chicago street gang, active in Chicago and New York City.
Revolutionary socialist organization that inspired the Young Lords.
Organization founded by Sylvia Rivera, focused on LGBTQ rights.

edges.evidence_quote: Under the leadership of José "Cha Cha" Jiménez , who was inspired by civil rights leaders and the Black Panther Party , the Chicago Young Lords allied themselves with various socialist organizations.
edges.context: The Young Lords allied with the Black Panther Party and adopted their Ten-Point Program.

edges.evidence_quote: The Young Lords occupied the Presbyterian McCormick Theological Seminary in collaboration with the Black Panthers, LADO, the Young Patriots Organization , and the SDS for almost a week
edges.context: The Young Lords collaborated with LADO in a building occupation.

edges.evidence_quote: The Young Lords occupied the Presbyterian McCormick Theological Seminary in collaboration with the Black Panthers, LADO, the Young Patriots Organization , and the SDS for almost a week
edges.context: The Young Lords collaborated with the Young Patriots Organization in a building occupation.

edges.evidence_quote: The Young Lords occupied the Presbyterian McCormick Theological Seminary in collaboration with the Black Panthers, LADO, the Young Patriots Organization , and the SDS for almost a week
edges.context: The Young Lords collaborated with SDS in a building occupation.

edges.evidence_quote: In June 1972, the YLP was renamed the Puerto Rican Revolutionary Workers Organization (PRRWO)
edges.context: The Young Lords Party was renamed and succeeded by the PRRWO.

edges.evidence_quote: some members leaving to join the Partido Socialista Puertorriqueño (PSPR, transl. 'Puerto Rican Socialist Party')
edges.context: Some YLP members left to join the PSPR after the YLP dissolved.

edges.evidence_quote: Under the leadership of José "Cha Cha" Jiménez, who was inspired by civil rights leaders and the Black Panther Party, the Chicago Young Lords allied themselves with various socialist organizations.
edges.context: The Young Lords were inspired by and allied with the Black Panther Party.

edges.evidence_quote: the YLP founded the "Think Lincoln Committee" (TLC) in collaboration with the Health Revolutionary Unity Movement (HRUM).
edges.context: The Young Lords Party collaborated with HRUM to form the Think Lincoln Committee.

edges.evidence_quote: The Bridgeport chapter developed in 1970 from a merger between the Young Lords and a local organization, the Spanish People in Command.
edges.context: The Young Lords merged with Spanish People in Command to form the Bridgeport chapter.

edges.evidence_quote: The Philadelphia Young Lords, who also emerged from a local group, the Young Revolutionaries for Independence
edges.context: The Philadelphia Young Lords emerged from the Young Revolutionaries for Independence.

edges.evidence_quote: Established nationalist groups like the Movement Pro Independence (MPI), led by Juan Mari Brás, criticized their perceived arrogance and unfamiliarity with the local independence movement.
edges.context: The Movement Pro Independence criticized the Young Lords Party.

edges.evidence_quote: Some members of the organization split off to join the Partido Socialista Puertorriqueño (PSPR, transl. 'Puerto Rican Socialist Party').
edges.context: Some Young Lords members split off to join the PSPR.

edges.evidence_quote: Under the leadership of José "Cha Cha" Jiménez, who was inspired by civil rights leaders and the Black Panther Party, the Chicago Young Lords allied themselves with various socialist organizations.
edges.context: The Young Lords were inspired by and allied with the Black Panther Party.

edges.evidence_quote: Sylvia Rivera, founder of the organization Street Transvestite Action Revolutionaries (STAR), was a member of the YLP.
edges.context: Sylvia Rivera, founder of STAR, was a member of the Young Lords, indicating a structural tie.
